news_summary: Recent advancements showcase groundbreaking discoveries in magnetic phenomena and significant progress in quantum physics driven by pioneering research.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes mettent en lumière des découvertes révolutionnaires dans le domaine des phénomènes magnétiques et des progrès significatifs en physique quantique, fruits d'une recherche pionnière.
news_summary_es: Los avances recientes ponen de manifiesto descubrimientos revolucionarios en el ámbito de los fenómenos magnéticos y avances significativos en la física cuántica, impulsados por investigaciones pioneras.

articles.title: Scientists discover bizarre new states inside tiny magnetic whirlpools
articles.summary: Researchers have uncovered a new way to generate exotic oscillation states in tiny magnetic structures—using only minimal energy. By exciting magnetic waves, they triggered a delicate motion that produced a rich spectrum of signals never seen before in this system. The finding challenges existing assumptions and could help connect different types of technologies, from conventional electronics to quantum devices. It’s a small effect with potentially huge implications.
articles.content:
Researchers at the Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf (HZDR) have identified previously unseen oscillation patterns known as Floquet states inside extremely small magnetic vortices. In contrast to earlier studies that relied on powerful laser pulses to generate these states, the Dresden team found that gentle stimulation using magnetic waves is enough. This discovery not only challenges existing ideas in fundamental physics but may also serve as a kind of universal connector linking electronics, spintronics, and quantum technologies. The findings were published in Science.

Magnetic vortices form in ultrathin disks made of materials like nickel-iron, often just micrometers or even nanometers in size. Inside these structures, tiny magnetic moments, which behave like miniature compass needles, align in circular patterns. When disturbed, waves ripple through the system in a way similar to a stadium crowd performing a coordinated "wave." Each magnetic moment tilts slightly and passes its motion to the next, creating a chain reaction. These collective wave-like excitations are known as magnons.

"These magnons can transmit information through a magnet without the need for charge transport," explains project leader Dr. Helmut Schultheiß from the Institute of Ion Beam Physics and Materials Research at HZDR. "This capability makes them highly attractive for research into next-generation computing technologies."

Unexpected Frequency Combs in Tiny Magnetic Disks

The researchers had been experimenting with especially small magnetic disks, shrinking them from several micrometers down to just a few hundred nanometers. Their goal was to explore how disk size might influence neuromorphic computing, a brain-inspired approach to processing information. However, during data analysis, they noticed something unusual. Instead of a single resonance signal, some disks produced a series of closely spaced lines, forming what is known as a frequency comb.

"At first we assumed it was a measurement artifact or some kind of interference," recalls Schultheiß. "But when we repeated the experiment, the effect reappeared. That is when it became clear we were looking at something genuinely new."

Rotating Vortex Core Drives New Oscillation States

The explanation traces back to work by the French mathematician Gaston Floquet, who showed in the 19th century that systems exposed to periodic forces can develop entirely new oscillation states. Typically, creating these Floquet states has required large energy inputs, often delivered by intense laser pulses.

In this case, the researchers found that magnetic vortices can naturally produce Floquet states when magnons are sufficiently energized. The magnons transfer some of their energy to the vortex core, causing it to move in a tiny circular path around its center. Even this small motion is enough to rhythmically alter the magnetic state.

In experiments, this appears as a frequency comb. Instead of one sharp signal, multiple evenly spaced lines emerge, similar to how a pure tone can split into harmonics. "We were stunned that such a minute core motion was enough to transform the familiar magnon spectrum into a whole array of new states," says Schultheiß.

Ultra-Low Energy Breakthrough With Big Potential

One of the most striking aspects of the discovery is how little energy it requires. While previous methods depended on high-powered lasers, this effect can be triggered with microwatts of power, far less than what a smartphone uses in standby mode.

This efficiency opens up new possibilities. Frequency combs generated in this way could help synchronize very different systems, connecting ultrafast terahertz signals with conventional electronics or even quantum devices. "We call it the universal adapter," Schultheiß explains. "Just as a USB adapter allows devices with different connectors to work together, Floquet magnons could bridge frequencies that would otherwise remain incompatible."

Toward Future Computing and Quantum Integration

The team plans to investigate whether the same mechanism can be applied to other magnetic structures. The discovery could play an important role in developing future computing systems by enabling communication between magnon-based signals, electronic circuits, and quantum components.

"On the one hand, our discovery opens new avenues for addressing fundamental questions in magnetism," Schultheiß emphasizes. "On the other hand, it could eventually serve as a valuable tool to interconnect the realms of electronics, spintronics, and quantum information technology."

All measurements of the magnetic vortices and analysis of data from multiple instruments were carried out using the Labmule program developed at HZDR, which is available as a lab automation tool.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of new oscillation states in tiny magnetic vortices that requires minimal energy and has the potential to bridge conventional electronics with quantum technologies. This breakthrough has broad implications for future computing and quantum integration, representing a meaningful advancement in technology with tangible benefits for society.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find new energy-saving signals in tiny magnetic whirlpools
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study tiny magnetic whirlpools called vortices inside very small disks made of nickel and iron. These vortices have magnetic parts that move together in waves, carrying information without electricity.

**What happened** – Researchers at Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf discovered new patterns of signals, called Floquet states, inside these magnetic vortices. Unlike before, they created these patterns using very little energy by gently exciting magnetic waves instead of powerful lasers. This caused the center of the vortex to move slightly in a circle, producing a unique set of signals called frequency combs.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows a new way to create complex signals with very low energy. These signals could act like a universal adapter, linking regular electronics, spintronics (technology using electron spins), and quantum devices. This could help different technologies work together more easily.

**What's next step** – The scientists will explore if this effect happens in other magnetic structures and how it can be used to improve future computers. They hope it will help connect different types of technology, making faster and more efficient devices.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers found a new, low-energy way to create complex magnetic signals that could connect electronics and quantum technology for future computing.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent de nouveaux signaux économes en énergie dans de minuscules tourbillons magnétiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques étudient de minuscules tourbillons magnétiques appelés vortex à l’intérieur de très petits disques en nickel et fer. Ces vortex ont des parties magnétiques qui bougent ensemble en ondes, transmettant de l’information sans électricité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs du Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf ont découvert de nouveaux motifs de signaux, appelés états de Floquet, à l’intérieur de ces vortex magnétiques. Contrairement à avant, ils ont créé ces motifs avec très peu d’énergie en excitant doucement des ondes magnétiques au lieu d’utiliser de puissants lasers. Cela a fait bouger légèrement le centre du vortex en cercle, produisant un ensemble unique de signaux appelés peignes de fréquence.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre une nouvelle façon de créer des signaux complexes avec très peu d’énergie. Ces signaux pourraient agir comme un adaptateur universel, reliant l’électronique classique, la spintronique (technologie utilisant le spin des électrons) et les dispositifs quantiques. Cela pourrait faciliter le travail ensemble de différentes technologies.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier si cet effet existe dans d’autres structures magnétiques et comment il peut être utilisé pour améliorer les ordinateurs du futur. Ils espèrent que cela aidera à connecter différents types de technologies, pour des appareils plus rapides et efficaces.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé une nouvelle façon à faible énergie de créer des signaux magnétiques complexes qui pourraient relier l’électronique et la technologie quantique pour l’informatique du futur.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren nuevas señales que ahorran energía en diminuidos remolinos magnéticos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Científicos estudian pequeños remolinos magnéticos llamados vórtices dentro de discos muy pequeños hechos de níquel y hierro. Estos vórtices tienen partes magnéticas que se mueven juntas en ondas, transmitiendo información sin electricidad.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf descubrieron nuevos patrones de señales, llamados estados de Floquet, dentro de estos vórtices magnéticos. A diferencia de antes, crearon estos patrones usando muy poca energía al estimular suavemente ondas magnéticas en lugar de usar láseres potentes. Esto hizo que el centro del vórtice se moviera ligeramente en círculo, produciendo un conjunto único de señales llamadas peines de frecuencia.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra una nueva forma de crear señales complejas con muy poca energía. Estas señales podrían funcionar como un adaptador universal, conectando la electrónica convencional, la espintrónica (tecnología que usa el giro de los electrones) y los dispositivos cuánticos. Esto podría ayudar a que diferentes tecnologías trabajen juntas más fácilmente.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos explorarán si este efecto ocurre en otras estructuras magnéticas y cómo puede usarse para mejorar las computadoras del futuro. Esperan que ayude a conectar diferentes tipos de tecnología para crear dispositivos más rápidos y eficientes.

**Resumen en una frase** – Investigadores encontraron una nueva forma de baja energía para crear señales magnéticas complejas que podrían conectar la electrónica y la tecnología cuántica para la computación futura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate, glowing spiral vortex made of tiny compass-like needles arranged in circular patterns, with softly radiating concentric waves resembling a gentle ripple or “wave” moving through the structure, surrounded by subtle harmonic frequency lines forming a luminous comb shape that symbolizes new oscillation states and energy flow within the magnetic whirlpool.

articles.title: How Anthony Leggett pushed the boundaries of quantum physics
articles.summary: After the passing of physicist Anthony Leggett, columnist Karmela Padavic-Callaghan remembers their personal connection with this giant of quantum physics, and explores the legacy of his enduring recipe for testing the edges of the quantum world
articles.content:
After the passing of physicist Anthony Leggett, columnist Karmela Padavic-Callaghan remembers their personal connection with this giant of quantum physics, and explores the legacy of his enduring recipe for testing the edges of the quantum world

Sir Anthony Leggett was a giant in the field of quantum physics University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign/L. Brian Stauffer

In my first year of graduate school, I briefly shared an office with a quiet, older graduate student. When we finally managed some chit-chat, I learned that he was “working on theory of glasses with Tony.” Two things became clear: cracking the physics of glasses was difficult, and I really ought to have known who Tony was. I met him soon enough. A polite British man in his 70s, he spoke with the cadence of a life-long teacher and an incontrovertible twinkle in his eyes. His full name was Anthony James Leggett: a Nobel laureate, a knight of the British Empire, winner of countless prizes, an expert on the ultracold denizens of the quantum world, and a theorist who co-developed an influential test for probing just where that world might end, a question he pursued for decades. He passed away on 8 March, survived not only by his family but by countless inspired researchers to whom he was, in his characteristically humble way, also just Tony.

Leggett was born in South London in 1938 and attended a Jesuit school where his father taught physics and chemistry, before pursuing a degree in classical literature, philosophy and ancient history at the University of Oxford. But the siren call of physics was louder than ancient texts and dead languages. He earned another degree, now in physics, and moved to the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign (UIUC) for his postdoctoral training.

At the time, UIUC was rich with physicists studying new types of quantum matter and materials, many of which only revealed their exotic properties when made extremely cold. From his past work, Tony was already conversant in the physics of the ultracold, but the time at UIUC brought to his attention the problem of a rare form of helium called helium-3. In his Nobel prize lecture, he recounted the time physicists John Bardeen and Leo Kadanoff came into his office to tell him about an ultracold helium experiment happening in the basement. Leggett set out to capture facets of that experiment with mathematical equations but got sidetracked. He abandoned the calculation but would continue to have an on-again-off-again relationship with ultracold helium-3 for the next decade.

Serendipity stepped in to pull him back to the study of this strange matter. One day in 1972, he was on vacation when rainy weather thwarted his plans to go hiking. So, he met with an experimentalist friend, Robert Richardson, instead. According to Leggett, what he heard that day changed his research career forever and led to his Nobel prize. Richardson described the results of a study of ultracold helium-3 where his team used an imaging method called NMR that baffled Leggett to such an extent that as soon as Richardson left, Leggett said he “sat down to try to construct a formal proof that given the generally accepted laws of quantum and statistical mechanics, the shift observed in the experiments simply could not occur”. In other words, he worried that by studying ultracold helium, Richardson and his colleagues may have stumbled upon a crack in quantum physics itself.

Within a few years, Leggett worked out that quantum physics was actually fine, but ultracold helium-3 really was unlike any ultracold system that had been studied before. Around this time, the ultracold realm was already throwing physicists for a loop. Make gases or even some solid materials cold enough, and they sometimes behave so strangely. For example, at a low enough temperature, the electrons in superconductors don’t repel each other as usual but pair up and carry electricity with perfect efficiency. In other cases, tens or hundreds of thousands of atoms subjected to extreme cold can all assume the same quantum state and effectively behave as one chunk of quantum stuff instead of as distinct individuals. This is how a superfluid forms, and why it has zero viscosity and can perform unexpected tricks, such as climbing the walls of a container. Was helium-3 a super-something as well? Leggett wanted to find out, and did so with rigour.

He developed a comprehensive theory of ultracold helium-3, a mathematical undertaking that revealed that it wasn’t just a single superfluid, but that its atoms could form several different types of superfluid. In describing this, he also discovered a novel form of symmetry breaking – a mathematical feature of the new ultracold theory that could explain the previously mysterious measurements from the lab.

Richardson had been awarded the Nobel prize for his helium-3 experiment in 1966, and Leggett’s Nobel, for the theory, came in 2003.

Anthony Leggett (left) receiving the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2003 Darrell Hoemann/The News-Gazette 2003

“I still remember the communal euphoria in 2003 on the day the Nobel prize was announced in the wee hours of the morning,” says Smitha Vishveshwara, who was my graduate advisor at UIUC. Tony moved to UIUC in 1983, and she came to work with him as a postdoctoral researcher in 2002. “He was such a caring, gentle, wise mentor, friend, colleague and inspiration for so many of us.” I can picture him sitting at one of the round tables in the institute for condensed matter physics theory at UIUC, which now bears his name, engrossed in thought but never too busy to answer a question.

And Tony was interested in so many more questions than just the mystery of superfluid helium-3. There was the study of glasses that that older graduate student told me about, but Tony was especially gripped by the idea that quantum theory may not apply to the whole world, and specifically that it may not work for large objects. Could all the weirdness of quantum physics – like a particle being mere clouds of possible properties when no one is looking at it – be restricted to tiny objects only?

Legget speculated about this in a 2003 interview following the Nobel prize ceremony, saying: “If we really do still believe [quantum physics] in the year 3000, then I think in some sense our attitude towards the physical world at the everyday level will be radically different from what it is today, because we will really have had to face up to this weirdness, which by that time I’m confident will have been amplified to the everyday level. I think it’s at least equally probable and perhaps more so, that…we will find that somewhere along the line quantum mechanics breaks down and some new theory, of which we can have at present no conception, will take over.” He said his personal hope was that exactly this would happen.

Finding the edge of quantum physics

Looking for this elusive line of quantum breakdown, he and Anupam Garg had formulated a mathematical test in 1985 that can be used to assess the quantumness of large objects. You can observe an object’s behaviour at different times, plug those observations into an equation now called the “Leggett-Garg inequality”, and discern whether the rules of quantum physics still have a grip on it or not. In recent years, Leggett-Garg experiments have been carried out on several systems, from particles of light to tiny crystals, and researchers are constantly pushing them to ever bigger scales.

Leggett’s questions about the relationship between the macroscopic world and quantum physics also seeded the experiments that were awarded the Nobel prize just this past year. “I heard him talk about this in the early ‘80s, and others did too. We took his proposal and turned it into a very good experiment,” says John Martinis at the quantum computing firm QoLab, who was awarded the Nobel for demonstrating that quantum effects can show up at scales as large as circuits made from layers of superconductors and insulators. Leggett already had an in-depth understanding of how such circuits could test the existence of macroscopic quantumness, which was great motivation for Martinis and his team to painstakingly build them in the lab, he says.

“I think it is fair to say that Tony could look at what everyone else dismissed as a minor glitch on a graph and recognise it as signalling something completely new,” wrote his former student David Waxman at Fudan University in China. “Tony was extraordinarily sensitive to what nature was trying to say.”

Anthony Leggett explains the famous Schrödinger’s cat thought experiment Department of Physics, University of Illinois

Leggett’s own advice to young physicists encouraged the same approach. “If there’s something in the conventional wisdom that you don’t understand, worry away at it for as long as it takes and don’t be deterred by the assurances of your fellow physicists that these questions are well understood,” he once advised. Then, he added that “no piece of honestly conducted research is ever wasted”, even if it ends up sitting in a drawer for decades before spurring some new idea.

I left UIUC in the spring of 2020, and even at that time you could still catch a glimpse of Tony in his office, working into his 80s. I truly believe that he never stopped listening to nature with that famous curiosity and care. I wish I could have looked at whatever studies were still waiting for their moment in his desk drawers.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significant scientific contributions of Anthony Leggett in quantum physics, including his Nobel-winning work on ultracold helium-3 and the Leggett-Garg inequality, which have broad implications for understanding quantum mechanics and advancing quantum technology. His legacy inspires ongoing research with potential large-scale benefits for science and technology.
articles.personality_title: Anthony Leggett’s discoveries helped unlock the mysteries of the quantum world
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Anthony Leggett was a famous physicist known for his important work in quantum physics, the science that studies the tiniest particles in the universe. He helped scientists understand how materials behave at extremely cold temperatures, especially a rare form of helium called helium-3.

**What happened** – Anthony Leggett passed away recently, and this story looks back at his life and work. Born in 1938 in London, he studied both literature and physics before focusing on quantum physics. He worked at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, where he developed theories explaining strange behaviors of ultracold helium-3. His research led to a Nobel Prize in 2003. Leggett also created a test called the Leggett-Garg inequality to check if quantum physics applies to large objects, not just tiny particles.

**Impact** – Leggett’s work changed how scientists think about the quantum world. His theories explained unusual experiments with helium-3 and helped prove that quantum rules might apply to bigger things than previously thought. This inspired many other scientists and led to new experiments, including some that recently won Nobel Prizes. Leggett’s ideas continue to guide research in quantum physics and technology.

**What's next step** – Researchers are now using Leggett’s ideas to test quantum physics on larger and more complex objects. They hope to find out if quantum physics always works or if a new theory will replace it someday. This research could lead to advances in quantum computers and other technologies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Anthony Leggett’s groundbreaking work opened new paths to explore the strange quantum world and inspired future discoveries that may change science forever.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les découvertes d’Anthony Leggett ont aidé à percer les mystères du monde quantique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Anthony Leggett était un physicien célèbre pour son travail important en physique quantique, la science qui étudie les plus petites particules de l’univers. Il a aidé les scientifiques à comprendre comment les matériaux se comportent à des températures extrêmement basses, en particulier un type rare d’hélium appelé hélium-3.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Anthony Leggett est récemment décédé, et cet article revient sur sa vie et ses travaux. Né en 1938 à Londres, il a étudié la littérature puis la physique avant de se concentrer sur la physique quantique. Il a travaillé à l’Université de l’Illinois Urbana-Champaign, où il a développé des théories expliquant les comportements étranges de l’hélium-3 ultrafroid. Ses recherches lui ont valu un prix Nobel en 2003. Leggett a aussi créé un test appelé inégalité de Leggett-Garg pour vérifier si la physique quantique s’applique aussi aux objets plus grands.

**Impact** – Le travail de Leggett a changé la façon dont les scientifiques pensent le monde quantique. Ses théories ont expliqué des expériences inhabituelles avec l’hélium-3 et ont aidé à montrer que les règles quantiques pourraient s’appliquer à des objets plus grands que prévu. Cela a inspiré beaucoup d’autres chercheurs et conduit à de nouvelles expériences, dont certaines ont récemment reçu un prix Nobel. Les idées de Leggett continuent de guider la recherche en physique quantique et en technologie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utilisent maintenant les idées de Leggett pour tester la physique quantique sur des objets de plus en plus grands et complexes. Ils espèrent découvrir si la physique quantique fonctionne toujours ou si une nouvelle théorie la remplacera un jour. Ces recherches pourraient faire avancer les ordinateurs quantiques et d’autres technologies.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Le travail révolutionnaire d’Anthony Leggett a ouvert de nouvelles voies pour explorer le monde quantique étrange et a inspiré des découvertes futures qui pourraient transformer la science pour toujours.
articles.personality_title_es: Los descubrimientos de Anthony Leggett ayudaron a desvelar los misterios del mundo cuántico
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Anthony Leggett fue un físico famoso por su trabajo importante en física cuántica, la ciencia que estudia las partículas más pequeñas del universo. Ayudó a los científicos a entender cómo se comportan los materiales a temperaturas extremadamente bajas, especialmente un tipo raro de helio llamado helio-3.

**Qué pasó** – Anthony Leggett falleció recientemente, y esta historia recuerda su vida y su trabajo. Nacido en 1938 en Londres, estudió literatura y luego física antes de centrarse en la física cuántica. Trabajó en la Universidad de Illinois Urbana-Champaign, donde desarrolló teorías para explicar comportamientos extraños del helio-3 ultrafrío. Su investigación le valió un Premio Nobel en 2003. Leggett también creó una prueba llamada desigualdad de Leggett-Garg para comprobar si la física cuántica se aplica a objetos grandes, no solo a partículas pequeñas.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de Leggett cambió la forma en que los científicos entienden el mundo cuántico. Sus teorías explicaron experimentos inusuales con helio-3 y ayudaron a demostrar que las reglas cuánticas podrían aplicarse a cosas más grandes de lo que se pensaba. Esto inspiró a muchos otros científicos y llevó a nuevos experimentos, algunos de los cuales ganaron premios Nobel recientemente. Las ideas de Leggett siguen guiando la investigación en física cuántica y tecnología.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores ahora usan las ideas de Leggett para probar la física cuántica en objetos más grandes y complejos. Esperan descubrir si la física cuántica siempre funciona o si algún día será reemplazada por una nueva teoría. Esta investigación podría llevar a avances en computadoras cuánticas y otras tecnologías.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El trabajo innovador de Anthony Leggett abrió nuevos caminos para explorar el extraño mundo cuántico e inspiró descubrimientos futuros que podrían cambiar la ciencia para siempre.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an ancient, open book glowing softly on a wooden desk beside a delicate, swirling cloud of ultracold helium atoms forming elegant, flowing superfluid shapes, with faint, abstract mathematical equations gently weaving through the air, all rendered in natural, muted tones to evoke the quiet curiosity and profound legacy of a pioneering quantum physicist.
